transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Sydney. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit uneasy lately. It’s like there’s this nagging thought in the back of my mind that things are going too well, and I’m just waiting for something to go wrong. It’s hard to shake off that feeling, you know? I don’t want to jinx anything, but at the same time, I want to enjoy what I have. It’s just... complicated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re experiencing a mix of unease and a desire to enjoy the positive aspects of your life, but there’s this underlying worry that something might disrupt that. It can be really challenging to navigate those conflicting feelings. Can you tell me more about what’s been going well for you lately?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I mean, there have been some good things happening. I’ve been feeling more connected with friends, and work has been going pretty well too. I’ve even picked up some new hobbies that I really enjoy. But every time I think about it, there’s this voice that says I don’t deserve this happiness. It’s like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop. I don’t know if that makes sense. I just find myself overthinking everything, trying to prepare for whatever might go wrong.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes perfect sense. It sounds like you’re really appreciating the connections with friends and the progress at work, as well as enjoying new hobbies, but there’s a part of you that feels unworthy of that happiness. That voice telling you that you don’t deserve it seems to create a lot of pressure and overthinking. How does that voice affect your day-to-day experience?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It definitely affects me more than I’d like to admit. I find myself second-guessing my decisions or hesitating to fully engage in things. Like, when I’m with friends, I’ll catch myself holding back a bit, just in case something changes or goes wrong. It’s hard to relax and enjoy the moment when I’m constantly preparing for the worst. I guess it makes me feel tense and anxious, which isn’t really fair to myself or to the people around me. I want to be present, but that voice just keeps creeping in.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to feel that tension and anxiety, especially when you want to be present and enjoy your time with friends. Holding back in those moments must be frustrating, both for you and for your relationships. It seems like that voice is creating a barrier between you and the joy of those experiences. Can you recall when you first started feeling this way, or if there are any past experiences that might have contributed to those thoughts?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not really sure when it all started. It’s like this feeling has always been there, lurking in the background. I think maybe it comes from past experiences where things seemed good, but then they fell apart. I’ve had moments where I felt really happy, and then something unexpected happened that turned everything upside down. I guess that’s where the fear comes from—like if I let myself feel too good, I’m just setting myself up for a fall. It’s hard to pinpoint a specific moment, but it feels like a pattern I can’t quite shake.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there have been several experiences in your past that have reinforced this pattern of fear and hesitation. The idea that feeling good could lead to something bad happening creates a cycle that’s hard to break. It’s understandable that you’d want to protect yourself from that pain, even if it means holding back from fully enjoying the present. How do you feel when you think about breaking that pattern and allowing yourself to embrace happiness more fully?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, it feels a bit daunting. The thought of breaking that pattern is both exciting and terrifying. On one hand, I really want to let go and just enjoy things without that constant worry. But on the other hand, there’s this fear of what might happen if I do. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to embrace happiness and being afraid of losing it. I know it’s not a healthy way to think, but it’s hard to imagine what it would be like to just... trust that things can be good without something bad happening. It feels like a risk, and I’m not sure if I’m ready to take it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel both excitement and fear about the idea of breaking that pattern. It sounds like you’re really aware of the conflict between wanting to embrace happiness and the fear of losing it. That sense of risk can feel overwhelming, especially when past experiences have shaped your view of happiness as something that can easily be taken away. What do you think would help you feel a bit more ready to explore that trust in the goodness of your current situation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe it would help to start small—like finding little moments where I can allow myself to feel happy without overthinking it. Maybe practicing mindfulness or just reminding myself that it’s okay to enjoy things without waiting for something to go wrong. I guess having some reassurance that it’s normal to feel this way might help too. It’s just hard to break that habit of always preparing for the worst. I want to believe that I can experience joy without it being followed by fear. It feels like a delicate balance, and I’m not sure how to navigate it yet.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting small sounds like a thoughtful approach. It’s great that you’re considering ways to practice mindfulness and allow yourself to enjoy those little moments without the weight of overthinking. Acknowledging that it’s normal to feel this way can also be a comforting reminder as you work through it. It does sound like a delicate balance, and it’s okay to take your time in navigating it. What are some specific small moments you think you could focus on to practice feeling that joy without the fear?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could start with simple things, like enjoying a nice cup of coffee in the morning without rushing through it. Maybe taking a walk and really noticing the surroundings instead of getting lost in my thoughts. I could also try to engage more in conversations with friends, really being present and not worrying about what might happen next. It’s just little things, but I think if I can focus on those moments, it might help me build that trust in feeling good. It’s a bit scary, but I want to give it a try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like wonderful and achievable ways to start. Taking the time to savor a cup of coffee, being mindful during a walk, and engaging fully in conversations can help you cultivate that sense of presence and enjoyment. It’s completely normal to feel a bit scared as you embark on this journey, but it’s also a positive step toward building trust in those good moments. How do you feel about setting a specific intention for yourself as you begin this practice?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Setting an intention sounds like a good idea. I think I’d like to focus on being present and allowing myself to feel joy in those small moments without judgment. Maybe something like, “I give myself permission to enjoy this moment fully.” It feels like a gentle reminder to let go of the fear and just experience what’s happening right now. I know it won’t be easy, but having that intention might help me stay grounded as I try to break that pattern. I’m feeling a bit hopeful about it, actually.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful intention, Sydney. “I give myself permission to enjoy this moment fully” is a powerful reminder to embrace the present without judgment. It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling a bit hopeful about this process. Acknowledging that it might not be easy shows a lot of self-awareness, and it’s okay to take it one step at a time. How do you think you’ll remind yourself of this intention as you go through your day?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could write it down somewhere, maybe on a sticky note and put it on my mirror or my desk. That way, I’ll see it regularly and it’ll serve as a little reminder throughout the day. I could also set a few moments in my day where I pause and take a breath, just to check in with myself and remember that intention. Maybe even share it with a friend so they can help remind me too. Having those little cues could make it easier to stay focused on being present and enjoying those moments.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are great ideas! Writing it down and placing it where you’ll see it often can really reinforce your intention. Taking moments to pause and check in with yourself is a wonderful practice, too. Sharing it with a friend adds an extra layer of support, which can be really helpful. It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for success in this journey toward being more present and enjoying those small moments. How do you feel about taking these steps moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of excitement and a bit of nervousness, but mostly, I’m hopeful. Having a plan in place makes it feel more manageable, and I like the idea of having reminders to keep me on track. It’s nice to think that I can take small steps toward feeling more present and enjoying life without that constant worry. I know it won’t happen overnight, but I’m ready to give it a try. It feels good to have something to work toward, even if it’s just one moment at a time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful and ready to take those small steps. Acknowledging the mix of excitement and nervousness is completely natural, and it’s great that you’re approaching this with a sense of patience for yourself. Focusing on one moment at a time can make the journey feel much more manageable. As you begin this practice, remember to be gentle with yourself and celebrate those small victories along the way. Is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think we’ve covered a lot today, and I really appreciate the space to talk about these things. I feel like I have some good tools to start with, and it’s nice to have a clearer perspective on how to approach things. Maybe just... keeping in mind that it’s okay to feel a bit scared while I’m working on this. I guess I’m just grateful for the opportunity to reflect on it all. Thank you for guiding me through this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful and that you have some tools to start with. It’s completely okay to feel scared as you navigate this process, and acknowledging that is an important part of your journey. Thank you for sharing your thoughts and feelings so openly today. If you ever want to revisit this or explore anything else in the future, I’m here for you. Take care as you begin this new chapter!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much. I really appreciate your support and understanding. I’ll definitely keep what we talked about in mind as I move forward. It feels good to have someone to share this with. I’ll take care, and I look forward to our next conversation.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Sydney. I’m glad you felt supported and understood. It’s been a pleasure talking with you, and I look forward to hearing about your journey in our next conversation. Take care, and remember to be kind to yourself as you move forward!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I’ll definitely keep that in mind. I appreciate your kindness and support. I’m looking forward to our next chat too. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Sydney! I appreciate your openness and willingness to explore these feelings. Take care of yourself, and I’ll be here whenever you’re ready for our next chat. Have a great day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate that. I’ll take care, and I hope you have a great day too! Looking forward to talking again soon.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you, Sydney! I’m looking forward to our next conversation as well. Take care and enjoy your day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I will. Take care, and I’ll see you next time!
